Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Indefinite quantifier noun phrase: article + quantity noun + prepositional complement; functions as a predicate complement or object.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A considerable amount of work, trouble, or effort, often more than expected.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A lot of work or effort.
Usage Contexts: Daily speech; farming; household tasks; workplace planning; friendly complaint.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The word “wheen” is widespread in Ulster Scots and Scots for an indefinite number or amount; the phrase reflects rural and working-life talk across Antrim, Down, and Donegal.
First Example Sentence: Thon byre is a wheen o wark afore the cattle can be brocht in.
Second Example Sentence: It’ll be a wheen o wark tae sort the accounts afore Friday.
Third Example Sentence: Ye’ve made a wheen o wark for yersel wi that broken gate.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Adjective-modified singular noun phrase: intensifier adjective + count noun; commonly used as a predicate nominal.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Something very funny, ridiculous, or unexpectedly amusing.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A very funny thing or joke.
Usage Contexts: Humour; storytelling; family talk; casual remarks; social teasing.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: “Quare” is a hallmark Ulster Scots intensifier meaning remarkable or very; “geg” is local speech for a joke or comic incident.
First Example Sentence: His yarn aboot the goat in the kitchen was a quare geg.
Second Example Sentence: It was a quare geg when the wee dog ran aff wi the minister’s hat.
Third Example Sentence: They thought the whole mix-up at the mart was a quare geg.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Imperative verb phrase: base verb + possessive object noun; fixed pragmatic command.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Stay calm, think clearly, and do not panic under pressure.
Usage Contexts: Advice; emergencies; sport; workplace pressure; family guidance.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The image of the “heid” as the seat of sense and steadiness is common in Scots and Ulster Scots moral advice.
First Example Sentence: Keep yer heid when the road is icy, an dinnae brake ower hard.
Second Example Sentence: The match isnae lost yet, boys; keep yer heid an play the ball.
Third Example Sentence: She telt me tae keep ma heid when the interview questions got sharp.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative imperative reflexive verb phrase: auxiliary negation + lexical verb + reflexive pronoun; fixed reassurance formula.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Do not worry, trouble yourself, or become anxious about something.
Usage Contexts: Reassurance; family life; hospitality; neighbourly speech; minor problems.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: “Fash” entered Scots through older French influence and remains in Scots and Ulster Scots as a verb meaning to bother or trouble.
First Example Sentence: Dinnae fash yersel aboot the dishes; I’ll dae them efter tea.
Second Example Sentence: If the bus is late, dinnae fash yersel, for we’ve time eneuch.
Third Example Sentence: Dinnae fash yersel wi thon gossip; it’ll blaw ower by mornin.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Simile adjective phrase: as + adjective + as + noun phrase; fixed comparative expression.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Very stubborn, contrary, or unwilling to change one’s mind.
Usage Contexts: Character description; family disputes; politics; humour; rural storytelling.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: “Thrawn” is a Scots and Ulster Scots adjective for twisted or perverse in temper; “wuddy” evokes tough wood or a wooden object that will not bend easily.
First Example Sentence: Da is as thrawn as a wuddy when he’s set on mendin the tractor his ain wey.
Second Example Sentence: The councillor was as thrawn as a wuddy an wadnae shift on the matter.
Third Example Sentence: Ye’ll no change her mind the nicht; she’s as thrawn as a wuddy.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Optative fixed clause: adjective/adverb + modal auxiliary + possessive noun phrase + intransitive verb; blessing formula.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: May you live long and prosper; a warm wish for continued household wellbeing.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: I hope you have a long and happy life.
Usage Contexts: Toasts; blessings; weddings; New Year; literature; cultural occasions.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The smoking chimney symbolises a warm, occupied, prosperous home; the saying is shared across Scots traditions and recognised in Ulster Scots cultural speech.
First Example Sentence: Lang may yer lum reek, said Auntie May, liftin her cup at the weddin.
Second Example Sentence: We wished the new folk in the loanin, lang may yer lum reek in this hoose.
Third Example Sentence: Efter the harvest supper, the aul farmer smiled an said, lang may yer lum reek.

Idiom (Ulster Scots): Mony a mickle maks a muckle
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Gnomic proverb clause: quantifier phrase + singular noun + present-tense verb + object noun; fixed proverbial structure.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Small amounts accumulate into something substantial over time.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Many small contributions make a large total.
Usage Contexts: Finance; thrift; teaching; farming; community fundraising; moral advice.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A long-standing Scots proverb used in Ulster Scots communities to praise thrift, patience, and collective effort.
First Example Sentence: Save the odd pound, son; mony a mickle maks a muckle.
Second Example Sentence: The hall fund grew slowly, for mony a mickle maks a muckle.
Third Example Sentence: She kept the wee scraps o cloth, sayin mony a mickle maks a muckle.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Copular clause with directional particle phrase: subject + be + away + prepositional complement; idiomatic predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: He is distracted, unrealistic, eccentric, or not paying attention to ordinary reality.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: He is daydreaming or behaving unrealistically.
Usage Contexts: Humour; mild criticism; school; family talk; storytelling; gentle teasing.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Fairy belief is part of Ulster folklore; the phrase uses supernatural imagery to describe absent-mindedness or impractical thinking.
First Example Sentence: He’s awa wi the fairies if he thinks that oul van will pass the test.
Second Example Sentence: The wean was awa wi the fairies, starin oot at the rain instead o writin.
Third Example Sentence: When Tam starts talkin aboot gold in the bog, he’s awa wi the fairies.

Idiom (Ulster Scots): Tak the road an the road’ll tak ye
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Balanced imperative-plus-future clause: imperative verb phrase followed by coordinated future clause; proverbial travel formula.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Begin the journey or task and experience will guide the next steps.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Start, and you will find your way as you go.
Usage Contexts: Travel; personal advice; storytelling; resilience; migration; learning.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in rural movement, market roads, and emigration memory, where practical action often mattered more than perfect planning.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Ulster Scot: /tak ðə roːd ən ðə roːdəl tak ji/
First Example Sentence: Tak the road an the road’ll tak ye, Gran said when I feared leavin hame.
Second Example Sentence: For learnin a tongue, tak the road an the road’ll tak ye.
Third Example Sentence: We didnae ken the whole plan, but we took the road an the road took us.

Idiom (Ulster Scots): Ye couldnae bate it wi a big stick
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative modal clause with instrumental prepositional phrase: subject + modal negation + verb + object pronoun + with-phrase; emphatic idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: It is excellent, unbeatable, or impossible to improve upon.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: It is very good and hard to surpass.
Usage Contexts: Food praise; sport; music; reviews; friendly enthusiasm; local pride.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The exaggerated image of failing to beat something even with a stick gives the phrase comic force in everyday praise.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Ulster Scot: /ji ˈkʊdne bet ɪt wi ə bɪɡ stɪk/
First Example Sentence: That soda farl was that tasty, ye couldnae bate it wi a big stick.
Second Example Sentence: For a dry day at the Balmoral Show, ye couldnae bate it wi a big stick.
Third Example Sentence: Her fiddle playin filled the room; ye couldnae bate it wi a big stick.
